basicInformation.name: Brendan R. Carter
basicInformation.biography: Research oceanographer specializing in ocean carbon cycle, anthropogenic carbon quantification, and ocean acidification. Co-chair of US GO-SHIP Executive Council and major contributor to GLODAP global ocean carbon data product. Developed ESPER (Empirical Seawater Property Estimation Routines) and TRACE (Tracer-based Rapid Anthropogenic Carbon Estimation) software tools widely used in oceanography. Extensive sea-going experience as chief and co-chief scientist on multiple GO-SHIP repeat hydrography cruises in Pacific Ocean. Expertise spans carbonate system measurements, quality control of global ocean carbon data, and emerging research on marine carbon dioxide removal strategies.

primaryEmployer.department: Ocean Climate Division - Ocean Carbon Project

technicalSkills: Neural network algorithms for seawater properties
Global ocean data synthesis and quality control
expertise.institutionalKnowledgeDescription: Critical expertise in global ocean carbon data quality control procedures, GLODAP quality assessment methodologies, carbonate system measurement best practices, and GO-SHIP program operations. Unique knowledge of consistency evaluation procedures for pH data across global datasets, internal consistency challenges in CO2 system variables, and development of novel approaches for anthropogenic carbon estimation. Deep understanding of relationships between data providers, laboratories, and international collaborators in ocean carbon research community. Irreplaceable knowledge of ESPER and TRACE algorithm development and validation.

criticalDatasets.role: Major contributor to quality control, pH data assessment, and carbon system consistency evaluation

criticalDatasets.knowledgeTransferStatus: Partially documented through publications and code repositories

factors: Mid-career (PhD 2011)
Leadership positions (GO-SHIP co-chair)
Recent major software releases (TRACE 2025)
Active research program with multiple postdocs
Strong publication record

successionPlanning.successorIdentified: No formal successor, but strong team in place
knowledgeTransferMechanism: Postdoctoral researcher training (multiple postdocs working on related topics)
Collaboration with international partners (Gruber, Lauvset, Olsen)
successionPlanning.mitigationStrategy: Continue expanding postdoc program, ensure co-authorship and collaboration on all major projects, maintain comprehensive code documentation, formalize GO-SHIP leadership succession plan, strengthen partnerships with international collaborators who can maintain GLODAP contributions

basicInformation.name: Global Ocean Data Analysis Project Version 2 (GLODAPv2)
basicInformation.description: Comprehensive global ocean biogeochemical bottle data synthesis product emphasizing seawater inorganic carbon chemistry. Includes surface-to-bottom measurements from 1100+ cruises (1972-2021) with over 1.4 million water samples. Data for 13 core variables (salinity, oxygen, nitrate, silicate, phosphate, dissolved inorganic carbon, total alkalinity, pH, CFC-11, CFC-12, CFC-113, CCl4, SF6) undergo extensive quality control with systematic bias evaluation and correction. Annual updates add new cruises and improve quality control procedures. Essential for quantifying ocean carbon sink, ocean acidification assessment, and biogeochemical model evaluation.

federalContext.primaryAgencyDivision: NOAA NCEI (National Centers for Environmental Information)
federalContext.federalMandateAuthority: NOAA ocean observing mandate, GO-SHIP program

scientificValue.uniqueness: Only comprehensive global ocean carbon data synthesis with systematic quality control
scientificValue.irreplaceable: Partially - historical data irreplaceable, but methodology could be replicated

criticalityAssessment.justification: Only systematic global ocean carbon data synthesis with rigorous quality control. Essential for quantifying ocean carbon sink (31% of anthropogenic emissions), tracking ocean acidification, and validating Earth system models. Decades of international collaboration and standardized measurements make historical data irreplaceable.

impactIfLost.scientific: Loss of baseline for global ocean carbon cycle understanding, inability to track ocean acidification trends, loss of validation data for models
impactIfLost.operational: Inability to assess effectiveness of carbon sequestration strategies, loss of ocean health monitoring capability
impactIfLost.economic: Ocean carbon uptake valued at hundreds of billions USD in climate mitigation - loss of quantification capability
impactIfLost.policy: IPCC assessments depend on GLODAP for ocean carbon sink estimates, loss would undermine climate policy evidence base

basicInformation.name: Empirical Seawater Property Estimation Routines (ESPER)
basicInformation.description: Software package providing empirical algorithms to estimate seawater biogeochemical properties from limited measurements. Includes neural network (ESPER_NN) and locally interpolated regression (ESPER_LIR) routines to predict phosphate, nitrate, silicate, oxygen, total alkalinity, dissolved inorganic carbon, and pH from salinity, temperature, and location. Accounts for anthropogenic carbon accumulation over time. Trained on GLODAPv2 data product. Used for quality control, generating property estimates for calculations, and providing biogeochemical context from sparse measurements.

scientificValue.climateDataRecord: No - tool for working with CDRs
scientificValue.uniqueness: One of few comprehensive seawater property estimation tools with anthropogenic carbon correction
scientificValue.irreplaceable: No - could be redeveloped, but unique approach

basicInformation.name: Tracer-based Rapid Anthropogenic Carbon Estimation version 1 (TRACEv1)
basicInformation.description: Software tool and global gridded data product for estimating anthropogenic carbon (Canth) and water mass age in the ocean. Allows users to obtain Canth estimates with one line of code from geographic location, pressure, salinity, temperature, and year. Modifies transit time distribution (TTD) approach for computational efficiency and accessibility. Includes gridded data product (TRACEv1_GGCanth) spanning preindustrial era through 2500 under multiple Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). Released 2025 as major advancement in making Canth estimation accessible to broader research community.

scientificValue.climateDataRecord: No - tool for producing CDR estimates
scientificValue.uniqueness: First computationally efficient, publicly accessible anthropogenic carbon estimation tool
scientificValue.irreplaceable: No - methodology could be replicated, but represents major innovation

basicInformation.name: NCAR Research Data Archive (RDA) Collection
basicInformation.description: The Research Data Archive contains a large, diverse collection of over 700 meteorological and oceanographic observations, operational and reanalysis model outputs, and remote sensing datasets to support atmospheric and geosciences research. The archive includes atmospheric, oceanographic, geophysical, hydrology, gridded analysis data, and climate model output products for use in assessment and impact studies. Registration is free and open to the general public. Nearly all datasets are free to all registered users; a few are restricted to certain users (e.g., university researchers). The archive is heavily used by the reinsurance industry and other commercial entities for risk assessment.

eventDetails.title: Trump Administration Announces Plans to Dismantle NCAR
eventDetails.description: On December 17, 2025, Russell Vought, Director of the White House Office of Management and Budget, announced that the National Science Foundation would dismantle the National Center for Atmospheric Research. Vought cited 'climate alarmism' as justification. The announcement threatens NCAR's operations, its Research Data Archive (700+ datasets), and employment for ~830 staff. Congressional opposition is strong, particularly from Colorado delegation. Legal challenges expected. UCAR has approximately 6 months of cash reserves. Potential motivations include political retaliation against Colorado Governor Jared Polis over Trump-Polis conflict regarding Tina Peters pardon.

basicInformation.name: Kathleen Elizabeth Baynes
basicInformation.biography: Earth Data Officer within NASA's Science Mission Directorate, Earth Science Division at NASA Headquarters. Previously served as Deputy Chief Science Data Officer (ca. 2021-2023) and Deputy Program Manager of NASA's Earth Science Data Systems (ESDS) Program. Systems Architect for NASA's Earth Observing System Data and Information System (EOSDIS), where she was instrumental in the evolution from monolithic on-premises ECS systems to cloud-native Cumulus architecture on AWS. Leads strategic vision for NASA's Earth science data infrastructure, including cloud migration, multi-mission data processing, and open science initiatives. Computer engineer by training (BS University of Maryland 2001) with extensive pre-NASA experience in video streaming (set-top box programming during dot-com era), emergency response systems (post-9/11 NYC Fire Department), and law enforcement data exchange (Raytheon). Describes her team as 'NASA's Earth science librarians,' responsible for making 20,000+ datasets findable, understandable, and usable. Advocates for national geospatial data lake, inter-agency partnerships with DOE for computing infrastructure, and multi-cloud redundancy strategy. Known for metrics-driven approach mapping NASA Earth data usage to Fortune 100 companies across sectors.

expertise.institutionalKnowledgeDescription: Holds critical institutional knowledge of NASA's evolution from monolithic on-premises Earth Observing System Core System (ECS) toward cloud-native architectures such as Cumulus on AWS, including trade studies, budget and egress considerations, and DAAC implementation models. Deep understanding of EOSDIS architecture, 12 DAAC operations, cloud migration strategy, inter-agency partnerships, user community needs, and long-term data preservation requirements. Key architect of NASA's cloud evolution strategy and multi-mission data processing initiatives. As Earth Data Officer and former Deputy Chief Science Data Officer, understands cross-directorate data governance, open science initiatives, AI investments (including geospatial foundation models with IBM and LLM-based discovery), metrics strategies for Fortune 100 usage analysis, and the positioning of NASA Earth data with respect to commercial cloud platforms and external users across major U.S. industries. Strategic vision for national geospatial data lake, partnerships with DOE for computing, and multi-cloud redundancy. Emphasizes alignment with administration priorities of resilience, efficiency, prosperity, and security, and advocates managing budget cuts by focusing on labor reduction rather than losing irreplaceable data.

people.additionalNotes: Katie Baynes describes herself as thinking of her team as 'NASA's Earth science librarians,' responsible for making NASA Earth data findable, understandable, and usable across more than 20,000 datasets spanning satellite, in situ, airborne, international partner, and processed partner data. She emphasizes metrics that map NASA Earth data usage to Fortune 100 companies across sectors (major commercial users identified), alignment with administration priorities of resilience, efficiency, prosperity, and security, and the need to manage rising compute and storage costs by focusing cuts on labor rather than losing irreplaceable data. She highlights strategic AI investments including geospatial foundation models with IBM and LLM-based discovery tools, and advocates for a national geospatial data lake and closer partnerships with DOE for large-scale climate modeling compute and with multiple cloud providers for multi-cloud redundancy. Never left DC area - born in Washington, grew up in Prince George's County, went to UMD College Park for computer engineering, worked through dot-com bubble bust. Career path: dot-com video streaming → Raytheon (NYC Fire Dept post-9/11, law enforcement data exchange) → NASA civil servant → systems architect → Deputy CSDO → Earth Data Officer.

basicInformation.name: NASA Earth Observing System Data and Information System (EOSDIS) Collection
basicInformation.description: NASA's comprehensive Earth science data collection managed through EOSDIS, containing 100+ petabytes of Earth observation data from satellites, aircraft, field measurements, and model outputs across 20,000+ datasets. Serves over 4 million users globally with 300 TB distributed daily. Data spans multiple Earth science disciplines including atmosphere, land, ocean, cryosphere, climate, human dimensions, and solid Earth. Collection managed through 12 discipline-specific Distributed Active Archive Centers (DAACs) across the United States. Includes data from NASA's Earth Observing System (EOS) missions, joint missions (e.g., NISAR with ISRO, SWOT), field campaigns, international partners, and processed partner data. Undergoing major cloud migration from monolithic on-premises ECS (Earth Observing System Core System) to AWS-native Cumulus architecture. Katie Baynes describes this as managing 'NASA's Earth science library' with datasets spanning satellite, in situ, airborne, international partner, and processed partner data made findable, understandable, and usable.

datasets.additionalNotes: Katie Baynes Strategic Vision and Context: (1) National geospatial data lake integrating NOAA, NASA, USGS satellite data, accessible to all Americans for efficiency, prosperity, and security; (2) Partnership with DOE for large-scale computing (leverage DOE supercomputers receiving Nvidia chips, reduce NASA compute burden, build American AI competitiveness); (3) Multi-cloud strategy (AWS primary via Cumulus, but exploring Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure) with multiple data copies for resilience; (4) Integration of observational and modeling data (currently separate); (5) Reducing barriers to data use through cloud-native formats, AI/ML (geospatial foundation models with IBM, LLM-based discovery), and improved interoperability; (6) Focus on impacts of Earth system on people where they live; (7) Metrics-driven approach mapping data usage to Fortune 100 companies to demonstrate value; (8) Emphasis on managing budget cuts by reducing labor rather than losing irreplaceable data; (9) Alignment with administration priorities: efficiency, prosperity, security, resilience. Major initiatives: ECS to Cumulus cloud migration, Earthdata Cloud Evolution, Multi-Mission Data Processing System (MDPS) Study, Web Unification, open science practices. Team described as 'NASA's Earth science librarians' managing 20,000+ datasets to be findable, understandable, usable. Archives evolved from 35 PB (2020) toward 250+ PB (5-year projection) with high-volume missions like NISAR and SWOT.

basicInformation.name: Cumulus Cloud Archive System
basicInformation.description: NASA Earth science cloud archive and workflow system designed as the replacement for monolithic ECS-based systems. DAACs run AWS-native Cumulus software stacks for ingest, archive, and distribution within their own AWS accounts under a shared architecture framework. Provides reusable, cloud-native components for DAACs to manage Earth science data ingest, storage, and distribution without each center maintaining distinct hardware data centers. Reduces 'undifferentiated heavy lifting' by standardizing common functions while allowing DAAC customization. Built on AWS services (S3, Lambda, EC2, etc.) with infrastructure-as-code deployment. Katie Baynes was systems engineer and architect for Cumulus development and maintains strategic influence.

datasets.primary_dataset_name: Ocean Carbon and Acidification Data System (OCADS)
datasets.description: OCADS is a data management system specializing in the management of ocean carbon and ocean acidification data within NOAA's NCEI. It archives chemical, physical, and biological observations from research vessels, ships of opportunity, uncrewed platforms, laboratory experiments, and model outputs. It also serves as a repository for GOOS biogeochemistry Essential Ocean Variables (EOVs) related to ocean carbon and acidification research, including oxygen, nutrients, transient tracers, and stable isotopes. OCADS now also accepts data from marine Carbon Dioxide Removal (mCDR) and Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) research.

datasets.accessMethods: Web interface, FTP, API

criticalityAssessment.justification: OCADS is a globally significant, long-term archive for ocean carbon and acidification data, supporting climate research and policy.
